question_text: A circuit has a current of 2A flowing through it when connected to a 6V battery. What happens to the current if the voltage is increased to 12V (assuming the resistance stays the same)?
correct_answer: The current doubles to 4A. When voltage increases and resistance stays the same, current increases proportionally.
explanation: According to Ohm's law (I = V/R), if voltage doubles and resistance remains constant, the current will also double. So 2A becomes 4A when voltage goes from 6V to 12V.
